Commissioner & Player Guide

NFL Survivor (Private)

Everything commissioners and entrants need to set up, run, and play an invite-only NFL Survivor pool — from picking a format to reading the final standings.

Contents

Jump to any section — this isn't a persistent sidebar, just a quick way in.

1What Is NFL Survivor?2Setting Up a Contest3My Entries & Making Picks4General5Picksheet & Eligibility6Pick Format7Lives & Revivals8Missed Picks & Auto-Pick9Tiebreakers10Groups & Design11Entrants Manager & Invites Manager12Leaderboards13Missed Picks & Strikes Quick Reference14Frequently Asked Questions15Glossary
1
Overview

What Is NFL Survivor?

NFL Survivor is a multi-week, invite-only, elimination contest. Each week, every entry picks one team (or more, in multi-pick weeks) to win. A correct pick means the entry survives to the next week; an incorrect pick costs a life. Run out of lives and the entry is eliminated — the last entry (or entries) standing wins. There are no points: surviving is everything.

The three building blocks every commissioner chooses

ChoiceOptionsWhat it means for the group
League / Sport ModeNFL only, College (CFB) only, or NFL + College CombinedThis guide covers the NFL-only format — a pure NFL pool for the whole season.
Pick TypePick Winners or Pick LosersClassic Survivor picks teams to win. A "loser pool" inverts it: pick the team you think will lose — if they win, you lose a life.
Scoring BasisStraight Up, Against the Spread (Splash-set), or Against the Spread (Commissioner-set)Straight Up only cares who wins. Against the Spread requires covering a point spread — either Splash's live odds or numbers the commissioner sets by hand.
📝 How Survivor differs from Pick'em
Survivor has no Confidence format, no Best Picks, no over/unders, no weighted scoring, no segments, and no weekly leaderboard. The competitive depth comes from survival mechanics instead: how many lives entries get, how many times a team can be re-used, whether multiple picks are required in certain weeks, and how the endgame resolves.

Quick facts

  • Invite-only — no entry fee mechanic built into the contest itself.
  • No cap on contest size. There is no maximum-entrants setting; a pool grows until the commissioner closes entries.
  • Multi-entry is supported: default is 1 entry per person, and the commissioner can raise the per-person cap.
  • Runs a full NFL season — Week 1 through the Super Bowl.
  • One season-long standings view — alive entries on top, eliminated entries ranked by how long they survived. No weekly leaderboard.
  • Most commissioner settings can be changed mid-season; tiebreakers can be changed at any time, even mid-contest.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What happens to an entry that makes an incorrect pick and has 0 lives remaining?

2. How does Survivor scoring work?

2
Setup

Setting Up a Contest

Commissioners build a Survivor pool through a short guided flow. Here's what they'll see, step by step.

Step 1 — Choose the format

From "Choose a contest format," the commissioner selects the Survivor tab (alongside Pick'em and Other), then picks NFL Survivor.

The Survivor tab shows NFL Survivor and NFL + NCAAF Survivor as format options.

Step 2 — Name the contest

The final setup step asks for a contest name before creating the contest. A one-time service fee applies when the contest starts, unless the commissioner sets up Contest Vault to waive it.

Step 3 — Land on the Dashboard

Once created, the commissioner lands on the contest Dashboard with a setup checklist:

  • 1. Contest created — done automatically.
  • 2. Set up entry fee collection — waive the service fee with Contest Vault (secured by LeagueSafe), or manage payments independently.
  • 3. Review contest settings — the contest launches with defaults that work for most commissioners; review them to fine-tune rules, entries, or lock times before players join.
  • 4. Send invites — send invites through Splash, track who joins, or copy the contest link to share anywhere.
💡 Contest Vault
Setting up Contest Vault lets players pay through LeagueSafe, waives the service fee, and works across all 50 states plus Canada with no geolocation required.
📝 League mode locks at kickoff
League / Sport Mode (NFL only vs. College vs. Combined) is locked once the contest starts, so confirm it during setup.
🎯 Quick Check

1. Which contest tab does a commissioner choose to build a Survivor pool?

2. On the Dashboard checklist, what is step 2 after the contest is created?

↑ Back to top
3
Player Guide

My Entries & Making Picks

Entrants manage everything about their entry — lives, picks, and history — from the My entries tab in the left navigation.

Reading an entry card

  • The ❤️ heart icon represents how many lives the entry has left.
  • Click the ✏️ pencil icon next to the entry name to rename it.
  • Withdraw removes the entry from the contest permanently — there's no undo.
  • Make picks opens the picksheet for that entry.
⚠️ Withdrawing is permanent
"This entry will be removed from the contest. You cannot undo this action." Make sure the entrant really wants to leave before confirming Withdraw entry.

Making picks

Selecting Make picks opens the picksheet for the highlighted week. The entrant sees:

  • Tabs across the top for every week in the contest.
  • How many lives (♥) remain, shown near the top of the picksheet.
  • The pick lock time for that week (e.g. "Picks lock: Sun, 12:00 PM").
  • A running count at the bottom — e.g. "1/1 pick made" — showing how many picks are still required for that week.

An entrant must click Save picks for a selection to actually register with the system — simply clicking a team doesn't submit it.

To change a pick before the deadline, click Edit on the entry card, adjust the selection on the picksheet, and click Save picks again — every change needs to be re-saved.

💡 Plan ahead
When Picks in Advance is on, entrants can submit picks for future weeks in one sitting — useful for planning around Team Use Limits so they don't get boxed in later in the season.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What does the heart icon on an entry card represent?

2. What must an entrant click after selecting a team on the picksheet for the pick to count?

4
Contest Settings

General

The General settings tab covers the contest's identity, schedule, and entry controls.

  • Contest name / Description — the name is required; the description is optional, up to 500 characters.
  • Set your schedule — Start Week and End Week define the first and last week of the contest. Default is Week 1 through the Super Bowl. Setting the End Week into the postseason is how a contest extends into the playoffs.
  • Maximum entries per entrant — defaults to 1; the commissioner can raise it to any number 1–250 (raise-only once the contest is live).
  • Entry deadline — off by default, meaning most Survivor pools have no deadline and players can join via the commissioner's link even after the contest starts. When turned on, the commissioner sets a custom date and time.
  • Lock entries immediately — a hard stop that instantly closes the contest to new players, regardless of the entry deadline. This is how most commissioners "cap" their pool.
  • Hide Future Picks From Commissioner — on by default, meaning the commissioner sees entries exactly as members do (only picks past their deadline). Turning it off lets the commissioner see and edit everything.
📝 No deadline by default
With Entry Deadline off, the platform still applies a technical deadline behind the scenes — the contest's last game. A late joiner inherits already-decided results; in a 1-life contest that effectively means arriving eliminated unless the commissioner revives them from the Entrant Manager.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What is the default Entry Deadline setting for a Survivor contest?

2. What does 'Hide Future Picks From Commissioner' control by default?

↑ Back to top
5
Contest Settings

Picksheet & Eligibility

This tab controls which teams and games appear on the picksheet, and how often a team can be reused.

  • Team Use Limit — how many times a single team can be picked across an entry's lifetime. Adjust with the +/- stepper. This is the strategic heart of Survivor.
  • Pick-Against Team Limit — caps how many times an entry can pick against (select the opponent of) a specific team. Applies to both Pick Winners and Pick Losers. Click the dropdown for more choices.

Clicking the dropdown arrow reveals every available option: Unlimited, or a cap of 1 through 5.

⚠️ Don't forget Playoff Reset
"Playoff reset — team use & pick history: When the contest reaches the postseason, reset team-use counters and pick history so members start with a clean slate." This toggle is easy to overlook, and commissioners running a contest into the playoffs frequently forget to check it — leaving entrants stuck with the same team-use restrictions from the regular season.
📝 NFL-only picksheet controls
Some Picksheet settings — like Select Conferences, Include AP Top 25 Games, Exclude FCS Games, and Select Specific Games — only apply to College (CFB) or Combined contests and won't appear in a pure NFL Survivor pool.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What does the Team Use Limit control?

2. What happens when Playoff Reset is turned on?

↑ Back to top
6
Contest Settings

Pick Format

Pick Format controls how picks are scored, which direction entrants pick, and how many picks are required per week.

  • Score type — Straight Up (picks are scored on who wins the game outright), Against the Spread (Splash-set), or Against the Spread (Commissioner-set, where the commissioner manually enters spreads).
  • Pick direction — Pick winner (classic Survivor) or Pick loser (a loser pool, where picking a team that wins costs a life).

Picks per slate

Use the +/- stepper to set how many picks each entry must make per week. Use 1 for classic single-pick Survivor.

When Multi-pick is active, entrants must make the configured number of picks each week and every pick is graded independently — but all picks in that week must be correct to advance.

Advanced settings

  • Flexible Multi-Picks — automatically converts a future week into a multi-pick week mid-contest based on a condition (e.g. "when more than 100 entrants are still alive by Week 2, that week becomes 2 picks"). Multiple rules can be added.
  • Bye Pick — gives each member one optional bye per season; if they miss a pick, the bye is used automatically and the entry advances with no penalty.
  • Picks in advance — lets members submit picks for future weeks, useful for planning around team-use limits. Shown as "Always on" in the product.
🎯 Quick Check

1. Under Straight Up scoring, what determines a correct pick?

2. In a multi-pick week, what happens if an entry gets one of its required picks wrong?

↑ Back to top
7
Contest Settings

Lives & Revivals

This tab controls how many losses an entry can survive, and what happens if a whole pool wipes out at once.

  • Entry lives (a.k.a. mulligans) — how many losses an entry can take before elimination. 1 means instant elimination on the first loss. Any whole number 1–18 is available from the dropdown.
  • Stop issuing lives after slate — a cutoff week after which no more lives/mulligans are granted, useful for restricting mulligans to early-season weeks only. Only shown when Entry Lives is greater than 1.
  • Contest revivals — if every remaining entry is eliminated in the same week, this auto-revives all entrants (keeping pick history, with no extra lives) so the contest doesn't die mid-season. Off by default.
💡 Editable mid-season
Entry Lives and the mulligan cutoff can typically be adjusted throughout the season if a commissioner needs to loosen the rules for a struggling pool.
🎯 Quick Check

1. If Entry Lives is set to 1, what happens on an entry's first incorrect pick?

2. What does 'Stop Issuing Lives After Week' do?

↑ Back to top
8
Contest Settings

Missed Picks & Auto-Pick

This tab configures what happens automatically when an entrant forgets to make a pick.

  • Enable auto-pick — automatically fills missed picks instead of leaving them blank. Off by default in Survivor — a notable difference from what many legacy Pick'em players expect.
  • Who sets the auto-pick logic — Commissioner sets one rule for everyone, or Each player chooses their own preference when they join.
  • Team pick logic — the fallback strategy: Home favorite (pick the home team when they're the spread favorite, falling back to home team in pick'em games), Away favorite, or Random.
  • Maximum auto-pick events — a season-long cap on auto-pick events per entry: Unlimited, or Limited to a specific count. Once the cap is hit, further missed picks cost a life.
⚠️ Auto Pick is OFF by default
Because Auto Picks default to off in Survivor, a missed pick with no Bye Pick available simply costs a life. This trips up commissioners coming from Pick'em, where auto-pick behavior may differ — call this out early when setting expectations with the pool.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What is the default setting for Auto Picks in Survivor?

2. When Auto Pick is enabled, what does it never do?

↑ Back to top
9
Contest Settings

Tiebreakers

Tiebreakers determine how the contest resolves when more than one entry survives to the end.

  • Split (co-winners) — the default. Every entry alive at the end wins, regardless of buyback history. This is how most people understand Survivor.
  • Single winner via tiebreaker stack — applies an ordered set of rules to resolve to one winner.

When a tiebreaker stack is enabled, the commissioner arranges rules in priority order using the up/down arrows, and can add more from the options below the list.

  • The first rule that resolves to a non-tie wins — later rules only apply if earlier ones are still tied.
  • Additional tiebreakers can be added: Most Slates Survived, Closest to Championship Total.
  • Tiebreaker settings can be changed at any time, even mid-contest.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What is the default Tiebreaker Resolution setting?

2. Can tiebreaker settings be changed once the contest has started?

10
Contest Settings

Groups & Design

Two more customization tabs round out contest settings: Entrant Groups for sub-standings, and Design for branding.

Entrant Group

  • Group standings toggle enables grouped leaderboards (e.g. Marketing vs. Sales).
  • Group assignment mode — Commissioner assigns groups, Entrants may choose a group, or Entrants must choose a group before joining.
  • The commissioner adds groups by name under "Contest groups" before requiring entrants to pick one.

Design

Commissioners can upload or paste a contest hero banner image — PNG or JPG, up to 4 MB.

🎯 Quick Check

11
Commissioner Tools

Entrants Manager & Invites Manager

Commissioner-only tools live below the Dashboard in the left navigation.

Entrants Manager

  • Filter entrants by status: All, Active, Missing picks, Removed, Paused, Notes, No notes.
  • Search for a specific entrant by name.
  • Each row shows name, email, date entered, number of entries, missing picks, and a notes field.
  • Click Download in the top-right corner to export the full entrants list.

Pick log

The Pick log gives a chronological, filterable record of picks by week (slate).

📝 Privacy by design
"Live picks are hidden — only locked weeks are shown." This respects the same Hide Future Picks setting from General — the commissioner can't peek at picks that haven't locked yet unless that setting is turned off.

Invites Manager

From the Dashboard's "Send Invites" step or the Invites manager tab directly, commissioners can send invites through Splash, track who has joined, or copy the contest link to share anywhere (text, email, social).

🎯 Quick Check

1. Where can a commissioner download the full list of entrants?

2. What does the Pick log show once picks are locked?

↑ Back to top
12
Standings

Leaderboards

Survivor has one season-long standings view — there is no weekly leaderboard, since there are no weekly points to rank.

  • Alive entries always rank above eliminated entries.
  • Among alive entries (when lives > 1), more remaining lives ranks higher. Entries tied on lives are equal co-leaders.
  • Eliminated entries are ordered by elimination week — surviving longer ranks higher.
  • Buybacks and revivals never affect rank — a revived entry is treated identically to a clean entry at the same life count. Buybacks-used is shown as informational metadata only.
  • In a 1-life contest, all alive entries display as equal co-leaders until eliminated.
💡 Filters available
Standings can be filtered by search, following, my entries, status (All / Alive only / Eliminated only), and by group when Entrant Groups are enabled.
📝 How the contest ends
By default, all surviving entries at the end split the win as co-winners. A single winner is only produced if the commissioner enabled the tiebreaker stack under Tiebreakers.
🎯 Quick Check

1. How many leaderboard views does a Survivor contest have?

2. How are eliminated entries ranked against each other?

↑ Back to top
13
Quick Reference

Missed Picks & Strikes Quick Reference

A fast reference for the most common Survivor support scenarios.

Missed pick (no submission by the deadline)

The fallback order is:

  • Bye Pick (if enabled and unused) — consumed automatically, entry advances.
  • Auto Pick (if enabled and under the Maximum Auto Picks cap) — the system fills the pick using the configured strategy.
  • Otherwise — the entry loses 1 life (eliminated if that was the last one).
⚠️ Remember
Auto Picks default OFF in Survivor, and there is no Auto Pick Forgiveness (that's a Pick'em-only feature).

Cancelled, postponed, or suspended games

Postponed, suspended, and cancelled are all treated as one state — "cancelled." ("Delayed" is different — a delayed game still plays and scores normally.)

TimingWhat happens
Cancelled before kickoffThe pick stays editable until the deadline — the entrant just switches to another game. No penalty if they switch. If they don't switch by the deadline, the pick grades as a loss.
Cancelled after kickoff (includes suspended mid-game)The pick is frozen and grades as a loss.

Auto Pick never selects a cancelled game. A commissioner can manually adjust picks after kickoff as a release valve in edge cases.

"I ran out of teams to pick"

In team-use-capped pools, an entrant can pick themselves into a dead end — especially with multi-pick weeks or pick-against limits. There are no automatic guardrails; avoiding dead ends is the skill of Survivor.

💡 The fix
The commissioner can loosen settings mid-season (e.g. raise the Team Use Limit) to get the pool out of a jam. Direct affected entrants to their commissioner.

Tie games

Graded per the Tie Game Outcome setting — Loss by default, or Win if the commissioner selected that option. Rare, but always worth double-checking in the contest's General settings.

🎯 Quick Check

1. What is the first thing checked when an entrant misses a pick deadline?

2. How is a game cancelled after kickoff graded?

↑ Back to top
14
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: Is there an entry fee built into the contest?

No — NFL Survivor is invite-only with no entry fee mechanic in the format itself. Commissioners who want to collect payment typically use Contest Vault (LeagueSafe) or manage payments on their own.

Q: Is there a maximum number of entrants?

No. There is no max-entrants setting — a pool grows until the commissioner turns on Lock entries immediately.

Q: Can I enter more than once?

Only if the commissioner has raised Maximum entries per entrant above the default of 1. That setting can only be raised, not lowered, once the contest is live.

Q: What happens if I join after the season has started?

You inherit the already-decided results. In a 1-life contest, that typically means arriving already eliminated — unless the commissioner revives you from the Entrant Manager.

Q: Can commissioners change settings after the contest starts?

Most settings can be adjusted mid-season, including Entry Lives, Team Use Limit, and Tiebreakers. League / Sport Mode locks once the contest starts.

Q: Does Survivor have a weekly leaderboard?

No — Survivor has one season-long standings view. There's no per-week ranking since there are no weekly points.

Q: What if my whole pool gets eliminated in the same week?

If Contest Revivals is enabled, everyone is automatically revived (keeping pick history, no extra lives) and the contest continues. If it's off, the week's result is resolved using the Tiebreaker settings.

🎯 Quick Check

1. Is there a maximum number of entrants allowed in a Survivor pool?

2. Can most commissioner settings be changed mid-season?

↑ Back to top
15
Reference

Glossary

Entry
One participant's pick set within a contest. A single person may control multiple entries if the commissioner allows it.
Life (Mulligan)
A buffer against elimination. An entry starts with a set number of lives and loses one each time it picks incorrectly.
Team Use Limit
The cap on how many times a single team can be picked across an entry's lifetime — the core strategic constraint in Survivor.
Pick-Against Team Limit
A cap on how many times an entry can pick against (choose the opponent of) a specific team.
Slate
The set of eligible games available to pick from in a given week.
Picksheet
The weekly screen where an entrant selects their team(s) for that slate.
Straight Up
Scoring based only on who wins the game outright — no point spread involved.
ATS (Against The Spread)
Scoring based on whether the picked team covers a point spread, either Splash-set or commissioner-set.
Auto Pick
A system-filled pick applied when an entrant misses the deadline, if enabled by the commissioner.
Bye Pick
A once-per-season free pass that automatically covers a missed pick with no penalty.
Playoff Reset
A setting that clears team-use counts and pick history when the contest reaches the postseason.
Contest Revival
Auto-reviving every entry if the entire pool is eliminated in the same week.
Tiebreaker Stack
An ordered list of rules used to resolve a tie among surviving entries into a single winner.
Entrant Group
A sub-group (like a department or division) used for grouped standings within one contest.
🎯 Quick Check

1. What is a 'slate' in Survivor terms?

2. What does 'ATS' stand for?
